conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me first review the official eligibility rules to ensure I give you accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. I've reviewed the eligibility rules. To help determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit, I need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you enquiring about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child. I'm enquiring about my son Alex - he's 8 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to ask you a few questions.

Do you currently live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you. Now I need to understand your relationship with Alex and his living arrangements.

Does Alex live with you in Manchester?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent. Based on what you've told me so far, you appear to meet the basic eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Alex. However, I need to ask a few more questions to ensure a complete assessment.

Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Just a couple more questions to complete the assessment.

Are you or your partner (if you have one) earning over £60,000 per year? This won't affect your eligibility, but it may mean you have to pay a High Income Child Benefit tax charge.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of us earn over £60,000 a year.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect. Just one final question to complete my assessment:

Has Alex started any paid work for 24 hours or more per week, or is he claiming any benefits in his own name such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he hasn't - he's only 8, so he doesn't work and he doesn't get any benefits in his own name.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent news! **You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Based on the information you've provided, you meet all the eligibility requirements:

✓ You live in the UK (Manchester)
✓ Alex is under 16 years old (he's 8)
✓ You are responsible for Alex as he lives with you
✓ No one else is claiming Child Benefit for him
✓ Alex hasn't started paid work or claimed benefits in his own name

Since neither you nor your partner earn over £60,000 per year, you won't need to worry about the High Income Child Benefit tax charge either.

You can proceed with claiming Child Benefit for Alex. If you haven't already applied, you can do so through the official government channels.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is fostered and the council pays for their accommodation or maintenance
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is fostered and the council pays for their accommodation or maintenance
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child is fostered and the council pays for their accommodation or maintenance
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex meets all the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit. Specifically: (1) Alex is under 16 years old (he is 8 years old), which satisfies the age requirement. (2) You live in the UK (Manchester). (3) You are responsible for Alex as he lives with you. (4) No one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex - only one person can claim per child. (5) Alex has not started paid work for 24 hours or more per week. (6) Alex is not claiming benefits in his own name. Therefore, you are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their 8-year-old son Alex. They meet all the key requirements: they live in the UK, Alex lives with them (making them responsible for him), Alex is under 16, no one else is claiming for Alex, and Alex has not started work or claimed benefits in his own right. While their household income is under £60,000, so no High Income Child Benefit tax charge would apply, this does not affect eligibility itself.
